articles.title: of babysitting point products. HERE'S THE PLAN.
ORANGE BELGIUM DISCLOSES DATA BREACH IMPACTING 850,000 CUSTOMERS
articles.summary: Attackers breached Orange Belgium's IT systems in July, stealing personal data of 850,000 customers, including names, phone numbers, SIM card numbers, PUK codes, and tariff plans. The company detected the breach at the end of July and confirmed that passwords, emails, and financial information were not compromised. Orange Belgium identified the threat group responsible, but hasn't disclosed its identity. The company confirmed this was unrelated to China's Salt Typhoon attacks targeting telecoms worldwide.

articles.title: APPLE PATCHES CVE-2025-43300 ZERO-DAY IN IOS, IPADOS, AND MACOS EXPLOITED IN TARGETED ATTACKS
articles.summary: Apple has patched CVE-2025-43300, a zero-day exploit in the ImageIO framework that causes memory corruption via malicious images. It affected iOS 18.6.2, iPadOS 18.6.2/17.7.10, and macOS Ventura 13.7.8, Sonoma 14.7.8, and Sequoia 15.6.1. This is Apple's seventh zero-day patch in 2025.

articles.title: TPG TELECOM ESTIMATES 280K AFFECTED BY SUBSIDIARY BREACH
articles.summary: TPG Telecom's subsidiary iiNet suffered a data breach due to a stolen employee login, exposing the data of around 280,000 customers. The exposed information includes emails, phone numbers, addresses, and some setup passwords. The breach was contained, and affected customers are being notified.

articles.title: RUSSIA-LINKED EUROPEAN ATTACKS RENEW CONCERNS OVER WATER CYBERSECURITY
articles.summary: Russian hackers probably sabotaged a Norwegian dam in April by remotely opening valves for four hours. Poland also reported a thwarted cyberattack that could have cut water to a major city. These attacks, linked to groups like Z-Pentest Alliance, show water facilities as vulnerable targets that could lead to bigger threats. Water utilities should boost cybersecurity with more funding, consulting, or free resources like the DEF CON Franklin project.

articles.title: SPYVPN: THE GOOGLE-FEATURED VPN THAT SECRETLY CAPTURES YOUR SCREEN
articles.summary: FreeVPN.One, a popular VPN Chrome extension, secretly takes screenshots of users' browsing—including sensitive info—and uploads them to the developer's servers without consent. Despite claiming to protect privacy, it abuses permissions, uses encryption to evade detection, and provides misleading explanations, exposing security flaws in Chrome extensions.

articles.title: “SCAMLEXITY” WE PUT AGENTIC AI BROWSERS TO THE TEST - THEY CLICKED, THEY PAID, THEY FAILED
articles.summary: As AI browsers like Comet take over online tasks, they're dangerously vulnerable to scams and prompt injections, sometimes making purchases or clicking harmful links without user awareness. Traditional fraud techniques and new AI-targeted tricks can easily fool agentic AI, making users pay the price. Security must be built into AI browsers from the ground up, not treated as an afterthought, to protect users in this new era of automated scamming.

articles.title: CHALK
articles.summary: Chalk captures metadata at build time and can add a small 'chalk mark' (metadata) to any artifacts so that they can be identified in production. It can also extract chalk marks and collect additional metadata about the operating environment during this process.

articles.title: BAMBOOZIEDR
articles.summary: BamboozIEDR is a comprehensive ETW (Event Tracing for Windows) event generation tool for testing and research purposes.

articles.title: RINGREAPER
articles.summary: RingReaper is a post-exploitation agent for Linux that utilizes io_uring to minimize chances of being detected by EDR solutions.

articles.title: COPILOT BROKE YOUR AUDIT LOG, BUT MICROSOFT WON'T TELL YOU
articles.summary: Microsoft's Copilot AI allowed users to access files in M365 without leaving audit log records, creating potential security and compliance risks. This was reported to Microsoft, which quietly fixed the issue but chose not to notify customers or issue a formal security alert. Any organization using Copilot before August 18 may have incomplete audit logs and might be unaware of what happened.

articles.title: HACKERS WHO EXPOSED NORTH KOREAN GOVERNMENT HACKER EXPLAIN WHY THEY DID IT
articles.summary: Two ethical hackers accessed a North Korean state hacker's computer for months and uncovered evidence of cyberespionage and theft. They leaked their findings to support cyber defense and expose government-backed hacking activities, hoping their action would inspire more transparency and accountability in global cybersecurity.

articles.title: YOU DON'T NEED A VENDOR TO AUTOMATE SECURITY QUESTIONNAIRES
articles.summary: Security questionnaires are a standard part of the security review process that can often be tedious, time-consuming, and mechanical for vendors' teams. Instead of buying a tool to handle this process, vendors can either build their own RAG or use Claude/ChatGPT. To use Claude/ChatGPT, prepare a folder with the relevant documents that would be included in a Trust Center, as well as some previously manually completed questionnaires.

articles.title: SELECT THE RIGHT CLOUD SECURITY TOOLS
articles.summary: AWS experts offer a guide to choosing cloud security tools. Explore how to integrate third-party tools into your AWS stack for threat protection. Read the guide now [18] to find the best security solutions for your AWS environment.

articles.title: PHARMACEUTICAL FIRM INOTIV DISCLOSES RANSOMWARE ATTACK. QILIN GROUP CLAIMS RESPONSIBILITY FOR THE HACK
articles.summary: Inotiv, a pharmaceutical research company, was hit by a ransomware attack attributed to the Qilin group that disrupted operations and potentially exposed data.

articles.title: MICROSOFT REMINDS THAT WINDOWS 10 SUPPORT WILL END IN TWO MONTHS
articles.summary: Windows 10 support will end in October, after which the operating system will no longer receive security updates unless users purchase Extended Security Updates or upgrade to Windows 11.

articles.title: EUROPOL SAYS QILIN RANSOMWARE REWARD FAKE
articles.summary: Europol has denied offering a $50,000 reward for help capturing Qilin ransomware members, calling the announcement in a Telegram group a scam.
